skill.name: docx
skill.description: Create, edit, inspect, validate, and review DOCX files using docx-js, OOXML workflows, tracked changes, comments, and document conversion tools.
skill.summary: Work with DOCX files through document generation, OOXML editing, comments, redlines, extraction, and validation.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits, and most network, weak-crypto, C2, and Markdown backtick matches are false positives from OOXML schemas, XML namespace URLs, and documentation examples. The audit confirms a real high-risk issue: multiple scripts extract untrusted Office archives with zipfile.extractall() without path validation, which can enable Zip Slip writes outside the intended directory. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but publication should wait until archive extraction is hardened.

high_findings.title: Unsafe archive extraction of Office files
high_findings.description: Confirmed true positive. The unpack and validation paths call zipfile.extractall() on Office archives without validating member paths. A malicious DOCX, PPTX, or XLSX containing ../ or absolute paths could write files outside the intended output or temporary directory during processing.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact extractall() calls are present on paths that process user-supplied Office archives. This is a known Zip Slip pattern and no member path validation is visible at these locations.

medium_findings.title: External command execution required by workflows
medium_findings.description: Confirmed true positive with legitimate purpose. The skill documents and invokes tools such as pandoc, soffice, pdftoppm, npm, sudo apt-get, and git. The Python subprocess usage passes argument arrays and does not use shell=True, but the skill still executes local programs on document data and should run in a constrained environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and are part of the intended document workflow. Injection risk is reduced because subprocess calls use fixed binary names and argument arrays, but local execution remains a meaningful risk factor.

medium_findings.title: Broad filesystem mutation during document processing
medium_findings.description: Confirmed true positive with legitimate purpose. The scripts create temporary directories, copy unpacked document trees, write archives, remove temporary directories, and copy edited content back to a destination. These operations are expected for DOCX editing but can overwrite files if paths are misused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are clearly present and necessary for packaging Office documents. The risk depends on caller-controlled input and destination paths, so confidence is moderate rather than very high.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are mostly OOXML namespaces
low_findings.description: False positive for active networking. The hardcoded URLs reviewed are XML namespace and schema identifiers used in Office documents, not code that sends data over the network. No evidence found of credential exfiltration or unauthorized HTTP requests in the reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited values are namespace constants or XML templates, and the reviewed code does not call network client APIs around them. This strongly supports a false-positive network verdict.

low_findings.title: Markdown backtick matches are documentation examples
low_findings.description: False positive for Ruby or shell backtick execution. Many external command detections are Markdown code fences and inline examples in documentation rather than executable Ruby code. Actual command execution is covered separately as a medium finding.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The excerpts are Markdown tutorials and code blocks, not Ruby source files. They can influence agent behavior, but they are not direct backtick execution in the package itself.

low_findings.title: Weak-crypto and C2 keyword hits are schema terminology
low_findings.description: Mostly false positive. The scanner appears to match OOXML schema terms, hash-related document fields, IDs, and general document terminology. No evidence found that the skill implements weak cryptography, command-and-control behavior, or network scanning in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed examples are document editing helpers or static schema content, not cryptographic implementations or network control logic. Some static matches were not individually inspected, so confidence remains below very high.

dangerous_patterns.title: zipfile.extractall without path validation
dangerous_patterns.description: The highest-risk pattern is direct archive extraction from Office files without checking each member path against the destination directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The calls are explicit and no safe extraction wrapper is used at the cited lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local external tool execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflows depend on local binaries for conversion, validation, and diff generation. These tools should run with least privilege and constrained input directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present and intentional. The use of argument arrays lowers command injection risk, but executing document converters is still a dangerous pattern for untrusted files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged package installation instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documentation recommends sudo apt-get install commands for dependencies. This is not malicious by itself, but marketplace users should not run privileged package installation from untrusted skill instructions without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are directly documented. They are dependency setup instructions, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Professional DOCX work often requires precise formatting, comments, redlines, and XML-level repairs. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows for creating, editing, validating, and inspecting Word documents.
actual_capabilities: Create new DOCX files with docx-js components, styles, tables, lists, headers, footers, images, and page layout.
Extract document text to Markdown with pandoc while preserving tracked change views.
Unpack DOCX files to inspect XML parts, comments, media, relationships, metadata, and document structure.
Edit existing DOCX content through a Python Document library and lower-level OOXML operations.
Add comments, replies, tracked insertions, tracked deletions, and redline-oriented review changes.
Validate and convert documents with local tools such as LibreOffice, Poppler, and schema validators.
limitations: Requires local tools such as pandoc, docx-js, LibreOffice, Poppler, Python, and defusedxml for full functionality.
Unsafe archive extraction must be fixed before use with untrusted Office files.
Direct OOXML edits can corrupt documents when element order, relationships, or content types are incorrect.
Designed for modern Office Open XML files, not legacy binary .doc files.

use_cases.title: Review a contract with tracked changes
use_cases.description: Apply precise redlines, comments, and replies while preserving the original Word review workflow.

use_cases.title: Generate a formatted business document
use_cases.description: Create reports, proposals, briefs, or letters with consistent headings, tables, lists, and page layout.

use_cases.title: Inspect and repair DOCX internals
use_cases.description: Unpack a document, inspect XML parts, validate relationships, and correct structural problems.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simple DOCX
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DOCX file for a one-page project update. Include a title, three sections, a bullet list, and a short next-steps table.

prompt_templates.title: Extract and summarize a document
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the text from this DOCX file, preserve tracked change information where possible, and summarize the key decisions and open questions.

prompt_templates.title: Add comments and tracked edits
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this DOCX as a business reviewer. Add tracked changes for unclear wording and add comments explaining each recommendation.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose OOXML structure issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Unpack this DOCX, inspect the relevant OOXML files, identify relationship or content type issues, repair them, repack the file, and verify that it opens correctly.

output_examples.input: Create a polished client proposal from my outline.
output_examples.output: Produces a DOCX with title styling, section headings, numbered recommendations, a pricing table, and consistent page margins.

output_examples.input: Review this policy document and suggest edits.
output_examples.output: Returns an updated DOCX with tracked insertions, tracked deletions, and comments tied to the relevant paragraphs.

output_examples.input: This Word file looks corrupt after editing.
output_examples.output: Inspects the unpacked document parts, reports likely relationship or XML ordering issues, and provides a repaired DOCX when possible.

best_practices: Run the skill in a sandbox when processing external or unknown Office files.
Keep a backup of the original document before unpacking, editing, or repacking it.
Validate every edited document before sending it to reviewers or clients.
anti_patterns: Do not process untrusted DOCX files until archive extraction is path-safe.
Do not skip validation after direct OOXML edits.
Do not use markdown line numbers as stable references for XML edits.

faq.question: Can this skill create new Word documents?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses docx-js guidance for creating DOCX files with common Word layout and formatting features.

faq.question: Can it edit existing DOCX files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can unpack the document, edit OOXML, add comments or tracked changes, and repack the result.

faq.question: Does it support tracked changes?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes redlining workflows for tracked insertions, tracked deletions, and review comments.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted documents?
faq.answer: Not yet. The audit found unsafe archive extraction, so untrusted Office files need sandboxing or a code fix first.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: Full use may require Python, defusedxml, pandoc, docx-js, LibreOffice, Poppler, and git.

faq.question: Can it work with old .doc files?
faq.answer: No. The workflows target Office Open XML formats such as DOCX, PPTX, and XLSX.
